titleStmt.title: Letter from James H. Hampton to Robert Chamers, 2 June 1916.
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James H. Hampton

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from James H. Hampton (1858-1931) to Robert Chalmers (1858-1938), the under-secretary for Ireland. In the letter Hampton complains about receiving a Roman Catholic pastoral letter which was forwarded by the Chief Secretary's Office, despite the fact he is a member of the Church of Ireland. He goes on to state that considering the position of the Bishop of Limerick, Edward Thomas O'Dwyer (1842-1917) who openly supported the Rising, the government should not be forwarding such letters, or at least keep them within the Catholic community. He goes on to state that the issue will be brought to the House of Commons if it occurs again.Sir Robert Chalmers was sent to Ireland as replacement under-secretary for Sir Matthew Nathan, who had resigned along with Augustine Birrell, his chief secretary, following the outbreak of the Easter Rising in 1916.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/5612/9484

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
2 Merille Queenstown, 2nd June 1916. Sir, I received today from the Chief Secretary's Office
a Pastoral letter on Thrift by the Roman Catholic
Bishop of Meath. I have not read the Pastoral
nor do I intend to do so. I consider that an
unwarranted insult has been offered to me, as a
clergyman of the Church of Ireland, in sending
me a Pastoral by a Roman Catholic Bishop,
I am especially since it was forwarded 'On His Majesty's Service'.
When I consider the attitude taken up by the Roman
Catholic Bishop of Limerick with reference to the
Sinn Fein inflammation and the recent rebellion, one
would imagine that His Majesty's Government would
have something better to do than to send Roman Catholic
Pastorals throughout the country, and that
they would at least confine them to the members of
their own Communion. If any such literature is forwarded
to me again, I shall take steps to have the matter raised
in the House of Commons. Yours faithfully, James H. Hampton The Under Secretary for Ireland Dublin Castle
